text: The patient is positioned for a laparoscopic transperitoneal right radical nephrectomy, with the head oriented to one side and legs to the other. The surgeon marks the line of the rectus abdominis muscle and inserts a 10mm port one finger breadth below the last rib. A triangular configuration is created with four finger breadths between the optic port and two working instruments. A retractor is placed lower and more cranial than the other instruments, and a 5mm port is used for suction.

The surgeon navigates the laparoscopic view, revealing a large tumor near the liver. The surgical plan involves retracting the liver to access the space between the vena cava and aorta, aiming to control the renal artery first. The grasping instrument is used to clamp structures, while the camera provides a view of the gallbladder and colon, which appears higher than usual, necessitating its release to locate the duodenum behind it.

The surgeon frees the colon from the tumor, revealing a large, mobile mass. The pedicle is visible on the right side, and the vena cava is identified nearby. The camera angle is adjusted to improve visualization.

The surgeon carefully frees the duodenum and colon to access the space between the vena cava and aorta. The scissors are used cautiously to avoid burning nearby structures. The kidney is visible, appearing lower than usual, with the renal vein and adrenal glands identified. The peritoneum is lifted gently to explore the surgical field further.

The surgeon attempts to dissect around the renal artery, carefully lifting the vena cava to gain access. The camera view is adjusted to center the image, revealing the lumbar vein on the left and the renal vein on the right. The surgeon proceeds cautiously due to the immobility of the vessel and the presence of blood obscuring the view.

The surgeon identifies the renal artery, noting its position on the right side of the surgical field. The surrounding anatomical structures, including a lumbar vein, are visible. The surgeon uses a grasping instrument, but its tip is not sharp enough for dissection, while another instrument is too sharp and poses a risk. The lighting conditions affect visibility due to the bright white color of the bipolar instrument.

The surgeon frees the right side of the renal artery, preparing to place a clip for control. The scissors are used cautiously to avoid bleeding, which is controlled with a small tamponade. The tumor is visible at the lower pole of the kidney, and the renal artery is clearly identified.

The surgeon places an EmoLock clip on the renal artery to stop the blood flow, ensuring the clip is positioned deeply. The assistant lifts the section to provide better access, and once the clip is secured, the section is released.

The surgeon confirms control of the input flow to the kidney, with the vena cava visible and the tumor located nearby. The gonadal vein is identified, appearing dark blue, indicating its thin wall. The surgeon attempts to stay external to the gonadal vein while seeking passage near the psoas muscle, carefully lifting the tissue to avoid damage.

The surgeon navigates around the large mass anterior to the vena cava, identifying the gonadal vein and considering whether to leave it attached or remove it for electrical reasons. The section is placed deeper to avoid superficial dissection that could lead to bleeding by damaging the fat and vessels. The mass is heavy, requiring careful manipulation to prevent it from falling and causing bleeding.

The surgeon uses bipolar and monopolar instruments to detach a small branch that ascends. The surgical field reveals the plane of the psoas muscle, with the kidney positioned unusually low due to the tumor located on the lower pole. The surgeon mobilizes the tissue plane between the psoas muscle and the kidney, noting the thin appearance of the renal vein. The renal artery is already controlled, allowing focus on controlling the vein without concern for input flow.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers behind the renal vein, using a Hemolock clip to secure it without visualizing the artery. The clip is positioned close to the vein, following its posterior surface, and pre-locked before sliding down to close. The surgeon ensures the clip does not pass through the vena cava or stretch the vein excessively. Another clip is placed on the patient's side before cutting, confirming the artery flow is already controlled.

The surgeon clamps the vein with a grasp to check for bulging, indicating pressure. They are positioned behind the vein, searching for the artery. A Hemolock clip is visible from the other side. The surgeon dissects around the artery, encountering thick tissue and possible lymph nodes, and cleans the area for better visibility. The adrenal glands are considered for removal due to the tumor size, but the tumor is distant from them. The vein is visible behind, and the surgeon decides to control the artery first.

The surgeon places an additional Hem-o-lok clip under the renal vein and prepares to cut, leaving one clip on the kidney side and two clips on the patient side. The lumbar vein is identified, appearing large as seen on the CT scan. The surgeon cautions against movement during cutting due to the potential danger posed by small vessels and lymphatics in the area.

The surgeon lifts the section and adjusts the camera to better visualize the surgical field. They encounter a hard structure, initially suspecting it might be an artery, but identify it as lymphatic tissue. The surgeon removes lymph nodes from the right side, following the psoas muscle, and notes the presence of lymph node tissue. They attempt to retract the liver for better access.

The surgeon attempts to mobilize the adrenal glands from surrounding tissue, noting their posterior position relative to the vena cava. The adrenal vein is carefully managed to avoid complications, while the liver is positioned low in the surgical field.

The surgeon inspects a small vein, considering the use of titanium clips for ligation. The vein is located near the vena cava, and the surgeon exercises caution to prevent bleeding, ensuring it does not retract behind the vena cava. The surgeon confirms previous coagulation and clipping of the vein.

The surgeon attempts to place a clip on a vessel, noting the difficulty due to its proximity to the liver. The camera is adjusted for better visibility, revealing the parenchyma of the liver instead of the adrenal glands. The surgeon then proceeds to free the lower pole of the kidney.

The surgeon observes that the kidney is already devascularized, with small bleeding from dilated veins visible. The ureter is identified, and a large mass is noted, causing difficulty in maneuvering instruments. A prominent vein is visible and requires coagulation. The surgeon attempts to navigate around the lower pole of the tumor, which is challenging due to its size and proximity to the wall. The colon is positioned favorably, not attached laterally to the tumor. The gonad is visible, and the surgeon plans to cross the ureter and gonad to free the posterior part of the kidney and tumor before releasing it from the wall.

The surgeon attempts to release the uterus from its attachments while pushing down on the large tumor. The colon is freed from its last attachment, and an Hem-o-lok clip is prepared for the ureter. The surgeon incises the peritoneum and mobilizes the kidney medially, preparing to finish the procedure as the bag is ready for specimen retrieval. The liver is lifted slightly using suction.

The surgeon adjusts the view to show the lower area, revealing that the kidney is not completely released. They carefully manipulate the section to avoid the liver and note a large vein, which is common with large tumors. The surgeon uses traction to manage the vessels from the wall, emphasizing that most traction does not involve vascularization. The procedure is nearing completion, with the remaining challenge being to place the large tumor mass into a bag once the kidney is fully released.

The surgeon completes the last attachment, fully releasing the kidney and tumor. An incision is made in the inguinal area, slightly above the iliac crest, to place the kidney in a retrieval bag. The surgeon uses scissors to open the bag, preparing to insert the tumor.

The surgeon attempts to maneuver the kidney into a retrieval bag, noting the bag is slightly too small. The main part of the kidney is inside the bag, but it cannot be fully enclosed. A drain is placed before removing the kidney. The surgeon uses traction to open the muscle without making an incision, preparing to extract the kidney through the incision. Care is taken to avoid tearing the bag during extraction.
